title: Letter urges ANWR oil ban
summary: Washington -- More than 250 environmental and wildlife scientists joined in a letter to President Clinton Tuesday urging him to permanently protect the coastal plain of the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge from oil development. "We are concerned that vigorous and persistent efforts to mandate oil development will be successful unless administrative or legislative action provides permanent protection for this national conservation unit," the letter said. Among those signing are famed ecological scientists Edward O. Wilson of Harvard University and Paul R. Ehrlich of Stanford University. A quarter of the signers are from Alaska. The letter comes in the midst of a presidential campaign in which drilling in the refuge has become one of the top environmental differences between Texas Gov. George…

title: Phillips cleaning up Kuparuk spill
summary: Phillips Alaska Inc. has diked one section of a spill area in the Kuparuk oil field and is preparing to flush the ground with warm water to lift salt and crude oil off the tundra, state officials said. "(The cleanup) is going very smoothly. We're ahead of schedule," said Ed Meggert, head of oil spill response with the state Department of Environmental Conservation. In one of the largest spills ever to hit the North Slope tundra, more than 92,000 gallons of saltwater and crude leaked from a pipeline Sunday night. Phillips personnel discovered the spill at 10:45 p.m. when the pipeline, which ran from Kuparuk's central processing facility to oil production pad 1B, had a sudden drop in pressure. title: Bill would extend energy tax credit
summary: Washington -- Alaska Sens. Frank Murkowski and Ted Stevens on Tuesday introduced legislation to extend a tax credit to promote more energy development. The Republican senators said the bill is important not only for the country, but will help promote development of billions of additional barrels of oil from Alaska's West Sak field in the Prudhoe Bay area. The Clinton-Gore administration for several years has been seeking to end the credit, which doesn't apply to any facility built after July 1, 1998, Murkowski said in a statement. The senator said it makes no send to end the credit when the country is 58 percent dependent on foreign sources of oil. title: Is the Steller sea lion Alaska's spotted owl?
summary: In the 1970s a rare, finger-length fish, the snail darter, famously halted work on a huge federal dam project in Tennessee. In the early 1990s the federal government put millions of acres of Pacific Northwest timber off-limits to loggers because of a bird, the spotted owl. Cutbacks in fishing areas for pollock to protect Steller sea lions pose a threat to the state's $1 billion bottom-fishing industry. (File photo by Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) Now, in Alaska, another animal is gaining notoriety because of its status as an endangered species. summary: BP is planning its most aggressive exploratory drilling effort on the North Slope in more than a decade, a top executive said Thursday. O'Keefe, exploration vice president for BP Exploration (Alaska) Inc., told a breakfast meeting of the Resource Development Council in Anchorage. "But there's potentially a significant difference between structure size and reservoir size" and the drilling will provide information on how much oil might be present, he said. title: 5 VACCINES PUT ON SCHOOL LIST
summary: The State Board of Education and Early Development voted Friday to unanimously approve five new vaccines for children, initiating the first major overhaul of the state's immunization code in more than two decades. Before students enroll for the 2001-02 school year, they must receive vaccinations for mumps and for hepatitis A and B, both liver diseases. The Hib vaccine is used to prevent contracting Haemophilus influenzae type B, one of the leading causes of bacterial meningitis in children; the varicella vaccine is used to prevent chickenpox. title: Alaska holds, gains funding
summary: Washington -- Several Alaska programs held their own or gained ground in the president's proposed budget. It includes $30 million for the Denali Commission, created in 1998 to build infrastructure and provide economic development for rural Alaska. That's the same as was proposed by Bush's predecessor last year, said Jeff Staser, the federal co-chairman of the commission. "I'm pleased this administration, as well as the last, has recognized the efficacy of what we're doing," Staser said. The Denali Commission has focused on rural power issues, bulk fuel storage and health clinics. Staser said he is also pleased to see the other federal agencies the Denali Commission works with fared well in the budget proposal. summary: Editor's note: About a year ago, we added Access, a magazine focused on the internet and personal computing, to our Sunday newspaper package. summary: Washington -- The Coast Guard, facing a $91 million budget deficit, has reduced air and sea patrols by 10 percent. Unless Congress comes up with the money to fill in the gap, the agency will have to scale back its patrols by around a third this summer, the Coast Guard's commandant, Adm. "The angst that we live through is not the right way to run an airline," Loy said Thursday. The Coast Guard is running in the red because of higher-than-expected energy costs and higher pay and benefits for the armed forces. title: Inside Alaska Business
summary: Red Dog ranked as high polluter Hardrock mining accounted for about half of all toxic releases from industrial facilities required to report pollution data, according to a new report from the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency. Alaska's Red Dog mine ranked No. 2 on the agency's list of top 10 mining facilities nationwide releasing the most toxic material. Last year, it ranked fifth. No. 1 on the new list is the Kennecott Utah copper mine in Copperton, Utah. In Red Dog's case, the "toxic release" that the agency is mainly referring to is the large amount of waste rock that the mine moves annually. The rock contains naturally occurring heavy metals like lead and zinc, which can be toxic to humans in excess amounts. Unless properly contained, the metals can leach out and cause contamination…

summary: BP Exploration (Alaska) Inc. and Phillips Petroleum Co. are doing a good job of keeping environmental promises made about their North Slope operations, the Department of Environmental Conservation said Wednesday. "Both BP and Phillips are off to a very good start," DEC Commissioner Michele Brown said, referring to the "Charter for Development of the Alaskan North Slope," a $17 million agreement reached in late 1999 before Phillips bought Atlantic Richfield Co.'s Alaska assets. Phillips completed the $6.5 billion deal in August and is fulfilling Arco's obligations under the charter, Brown said. title: Siberian husky teams running a race within a race
summary: Willow -- The big race to Nome officially started Sunday when 68 sled dog teams bolted away without a hitch under a blue sky. Spectators debated which of the big name stars would win this year's Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race: Jeff King maybe, or Rick Swenson, or last year's record-setting champion, Doug Swingley. title: ANWR: RUMOR AND DESIRE
summary: What two words send a chill through Alaska oil executives? Greenpeace? Sorry, that's one word. Try: National monument. On Oct. 19, a boom that sucks toxic vapors off crude oil during loading was moving toward the deck of the tanker Polar Alaska. As the boom touched the deck, a spark flashed. title: Pipeline firm nixes extras
summary: Officials with the company that runs the 800-mile trans-Alaska oil pipeline and tanker port in Valdez have decided they will not install additional equipment to capture toxic vapors released during oil loading. "We'll continue to look at it, but the answer at this point is 'no.' We're confident that we can handle it" with existing equipment, said Alyeska Pipeline Service Co. spokesman Tim Woolston. Alyeska has been under pressure from a citizen's group that watchdogs tanker shipping and the Valdez port to install the vapor recovery equipment to cut air pollution. When Alyeska first came under scrutiny from the federal Environmental Protection Agency to collect the fumes, the company argued that vapor collection equipment on two of the four active loading berths would be enough. The company…

title: Alyeska practices criticized
summary: A report released Tuesday by an agency investigating the death of a worker at the Valdez tanker port blasted maintenance and work procedures that led to the fatal truck accident in August. Jerry Barnes, 51, of Valdez died Aug. 16 when the gravel-laden truck, weighing 30 tons, rolled off a steep road above the oil port and over a cliff. Last month Alyeska Pipeline Service Co., which runs the port, said its investigators found that brakes failed on five of the truck's six wheels. Also, the road was closed to heavy equipment like the truck, but no warning signs were in place. The Joint Pipeline Office, comprised of state and federal regulators that oversee Alyeska, has been investigating the accident. Its report Tuesday confirms Alyeska's findings but goes further, faulting company policies t…

summary: | | Predator proposal endorsed PROGRESS : Knowles backs plan that includes killing wolves, bears. By S.J. Komarnitsky Anchorage Daily News (Published March 3, 2001) Gov. Tony Knowles, who took office six years ago halting a state-sponsored wolf control program, gave his blessing Friday to a plan that could lead to killing bears and wolves to protect moose in the Interior region around McGrath. But don't expect any predator control soon. Knowles first wants an independent scientific review of the state's data on moose, wolves and bears in the McGrath area. He also said the state should first try to grow more moose by encouraging locals to kill more wolves and bears and by further restricting moose hunts. "The understanding is that we are not waging war on wolves," he said in an interview. "…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Judge rules again for Alaska Pulp in contract dispute TIMBER: Court finds that Forest Service erred in modifying contract By Dan Joling The Associated Press (Published February 17, 2001) Anchorage -- Alaska Pulp Co. has won another round in its ongoing claim of damages over the loss of its timber contract with the U.S. Forest Service. The U.S. Court of Federal Claims ruled Wednesday that the Forest Service erred in modifying the company's 50-year timber contract, due to run through 2011, following passage of the Tongass Timber Reform Act in 1990. The decision follows an initial ruling favorable to the company by Judge Lawrence Baskir in May. A self-described "serial entrepreneur." Someone who starts and sells businesses for the adrenaline rush, the art of the deal and, of course, the money. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Solo: Modern adventurer traces early Arctic explorers' footsteps By Natalie Phillips Anchorage Daily News (Published April 8, 2001) Wandering in circles on the snow-crusted tundra somewhere between Prudhoe Bay and Kaktovik in the spring of 1992, then 64-year-old Dick Griffith gave into the anguish Arctic explorers have known since the days of Peary and Shackleton. One of his busted wooden skis was mended with duct tape. He just discovered that his snow saw had rubbed against his fuel can causing him to lose most of his cookstove gas. Temperatures hovered around 15-below. "Some days I hate the Arctic, and this is one of them," the Anchorage adventurer wrote in his journal that night. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Zinc mine violations detected RED DOG: State alleges Cominco Alaska is responsible for multiple air pollution infractions. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published April 10, 2001) A slew of air pollution violations were detected at the Red Dog mine near Kotzebue, according to state regulators. Alaska Department of Environmental Conservation officials say they uncovered 18 infractions during an inspection last fall. The allegations include knowingly operating equipment that exceeded emissions limits, failing to report the pollution violations and failing to conduct monitoring, the agency says. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Halibut fishermen put on notice as flatfish farming gains FARMS: As with salmon, long-liners will face aquaculture competition. The Associated Press (Published March 8, 2001) Juneau -- Halibut farms haven't grown as quickly as predicted, but they are gaining ground and could eventually do to long-liners what salmon farms have done to trollers and other fishermen, an expert in the field says. "I think it's fairly certain in the next five years you won't have dramatic competition," John Forster, who authored a 1999 report on the emerging industry, told the Alaska Seafood Marketing Institutes board of directors on Tuesday. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Gift adds acreage to Tongass Forest DONATION Conservation groups turn Yakobi Island land over to the Forest Service. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published March 16, 2001) There's a little more wilderness in the Tongass National Forest now that conservationists have purchased some private land in Southeast and donated it to the Forest Service. The Nature Conservancy and The Trust for Public Land paid $82,500 for patented mining claims on Yakobi Island, on the outer coast of the panhandle west of Juneau, said Kent Whitehead, project manager for The Trust. title: Neighbors escape with clothes on their backs
summary: More than 80 people were forced from their homes Tuesday morning as a dramatic fire swept through a 20-unit apartment complex in Mountain View. Firefighters check the damage to the rear of an apartment building on Richmond Avenue where a two-alarm fire gutted several apartments and chased residents into the street, some barefoot and coatless. (Fran Durner / Anchorage Daily News) The fire spread so quickly that many residents -- mostly immigrants from Laos or Thailand -- left their apartments with just the clothes they were wearing. Many had no shoes on and stood out in the cold for hours in bare feet. title: Voters say no to Gambell extension
summary: With all of precincts counted early today, voters were said yes to paying the bill for $32 million in local road improvements, but rejected Mayor George Wuerchs proposition to extend Gambell and Ingra streets. Proposition 10, a bond issue to finance an array of street repairs and improvements to storm drains, traffic lights and signs all over town, cruised comfortably to approval by a margin of 2-to-1. Voters turned down, by a greater margin, Proposition 11, which asked them to foot the $9 million cost of extending Gambell and Ingra streets to the Ship Creek railyard. Wuerch pushed hard for the extension, arguing it is needed now to divert heavy truck traffic to and from the Port of Anchorage away from congested downtown streets. summary: Kaktovik -- Interior Secretary Gale Norton received a warm welcome Saturday from residents living in the only village within the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge -- a visit that proponents hope will strengthen efforts to drill for oil in the refuge. The delegation from Washington, D.C., which consisted of two Democratic senators and a White House official, was led by Sen. Frank Murkowski, R-Alaska, chairman of the Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee and a strong proponent of drilling in the refuge's coastal plain. The senators joining him were Jeff Bingaman, the top committee Democrat who has stated he is opposed to drilling in the refuge, and Mark Dayton of Minnesota. title: HIP-HOP RADIO STATION PROVES QUICK SUCCESS WAS NO FLUKE
summary: KFAT-FM, the upstart radio station with the hip-hop groove, is still the No. 1 radio station in Anchorage, according to the latest numbers from a radio rating agency. After being on the air less than a year, the station bounced up to the No. 1 slot last winter in the broad category of people 12 years old-plus listening between 6 a.m. and midnight. The station, at 92.9 on the dial, retained its No. 1 slot with a 10.9 share, or almost 11 percent of Anchorage listeners tuning into the station. The spring Arbitron rating legitimizes the station's No. 1 spot and proves the station's earlier numbers weren't a fluke, said Mike Porcaro, owner of Porcaro Communications, a public relations and advertising firm in Anchorage. summary: The 2-inch trout had just been dropped into the aquarium holding two Northern pike captured last fall in Cheney Lake when, faster than an eye blink, a foot-long pike snatched one. In a frenzy, the pike swallows a group of fingerlings. "We're just trying to educate the public about what these things can do," biologist Barry Stratton says. title: Assembly leads in budget power battle
summary: Anchorage voters apparently gave the Assembly more power in determining the city budget, loosening the mayor's grip on the purse strings. With all of the precincts counted, a charter amendment that would give the Anchorage Assembly final say over the city budget was passing by 396 votes out of more than 37,000 cast. The proposition was placed on the ballot with the support of eight of the 11 Assembly members. The impetus for it was the veto of the two most recent Assembly-approved city budgets by Mayor George Wuerch and then Mayor Rick Mystrom. When the Assembly was unable to override either veto, Mystrom and Wuerch imposed their own budget proposals. title: Vision center in flux
summary: When Sandra Crawford from Palmer had laser-eye surgery at the Lasik Vision center in Anchorage last month, she was expecting nearly perfect vision, not blundering confusion. Everything seemed fine until Crawford was scheduled to return a week later for a follow-up examination. Two days before her check-up, a woman claiming to represent Canada-based Lasik Vision Corp. phoned Crawford and canceled the appointment because, she said, the company was going out of business. "The first thing that went through my mind was that something must have went awry in their technique or procedures," Crawford said. But the puzzling call had nothing to do with her surgery. title: Shooting rattles motel manager
summary: John Yun's head hurts, and he has trouble sleeping. But he's alive. Yun, a 36-year-old motel manager, said he can't stop thinking about the man he shot early Tuesday morning from behind the counter in the Spenard Motel on Spenard Road. He thinks about what happened over and over, worries whether the police will catch other robbery suspects he didn't shoot, and tries to talk about it. Yun, the motel's manager and the lone clerk early Tuesday, remembers it like this: A young man wearing a mask rushed into the office about 3:30 a.m. waving a gun. He pointed it at Yun and demanded the money from the cash register. "He said give me all the money," Yun said, fighting back tears. "He threw a gym bag down on the counter and said he wanted the whole thing." Yun said it was the third robbery at the …

title: Born-again pair among missing
summary: Jim Mills and Shawn Bouchard were fishing en route to a higher calling. Family members described the two friends -- Montana natives who worked as processors on the Arctic Rose -- as born-again Christians who planned to pay off debts and then go into the ministry. Instead, family members believe, God called them home in the chilly Bering Sea. "I'm sure as their boat went down, probably everybody got saved," Chuck Mills said Tuesday by telephone. "I know they ministered to every single person. I'm convinced as it went down there was pandemonium, hallelujah revelations on that boat." Mills and Bouchard are among 14 crewmen of the fishing vessel Arctic Rose missing since the boat sent a distress signal early Monday morning. The body of skipper David M. Rundall has been recovered. title: Veneer plant not done
summary: Ketchikan -- The Ketchikan Gateway Borough Assembly has voted unanimously to take up the question of providing interim financing to the struggling Gateway Forest Products Inc. The assembly will hold a special meeting Wednesday to discuss the financial future of the veneer plant, and whether the local government should assume debts owed to local vendors who suffered losses in their dealings with Gateway.
